what_it_does: Defines CSS custom properties, @keyframes, and class-based animation bindings for directional screen-to-screen transitions across the Counterpart app. It covers slide-off/slide-on choreography for Welcome, Wizard, and Studio screens, a 'shed' animation for sub-parts dropped during shared-element morphs, and fixed overlay layers for the leaving-screen ghost and FlipBridge traveling clones.
observations: Custom properties (--scr-exit-dur, --scr-enter-dur, --scr-flip-dur, --scr-fly, --scr-stagger, --scr-ease, --scr-ease-exit) centralize timing, distance, and easing tunability.
Main slide animations are transform-only and time-based so they self-complete even if frames are dropped or the tab is backgrounded.
The leaving screen is kept as a transparent .screen-ghost overlay while the incoming screen slides in underneath, avoiding a simple crossfade.
Shared elements such as the composer bar and Vi orb are lifted into a high-z-index .flip-layer so they can travel between layouts via FlipBridge.
A .flip-freeze utility disables all animations and transitions so getBoundingClientRect reads true resting positions.
Dedicated 'shed' keyframes animate sub-parts that have no counterpart in the destination form, fading them mid-flight while carrying them in the parent's direction.
Each screen has paired enter and exit classes with staggered delays (welcome, wizard, studio).
A prefers-reduced-motion block resolves entrances instantly and replaces exits with a short fade.
standouts: The 'shed' concept: sub-parts that disappear during a morph travel with their parent and dissolve mid-flight rather than blinking out.
The transparent screen-ghost overlay model that lets old content slide off while new content slides on at the same time.
Heavy reliance on CSS variables to make the whole transition feel tunable from a single 'Tweaks' surface.
Explicit robustness goal: transform-only, time-based keyframes that cannot strand a screen blank.
conventions: Follows semantic, direction-based keyframe naming (scr-off-up, scr-on-from-below, scr-shed-left).
Uses BEM-ish class names (welcome__hello, wizard__top, studio-enter).
Centralizes tunable values in :root CSS custom properties.
Includes a reduced-motion media query for accessibility.
Breaks its own 'transform-only' headline by using opacity in scr-fade, scr-shed, scr-on-from-right, and scr-pop.
Uses magic-number viewport distances (115vh, 118%, 72vh, 64px) without deriving them from the custom properties.
concerns: Opacity is still used for several animations despite the stated transform-only robustness goal.
Hard-coded vh/vw/percentage travel distances may look wrong under extreme aspect ratios, zoom, or dynamic toolbars.
Magic-number z-index values (70, 120) are not tied to a documented stacking scale.
The reduced-motion exit rule applies scr-fade to all children of exiting screens, which still changes visual state and may override more nuanced instant transitions.
It is unclear from CSS alone how .flip-freeze is synchronized with JS measurement and how the flip-layer clones are inserted/removed.
notes: This is the motion vocabulary for the app's screen transitions: directional slides, shared-element flights, and a shed pattern for morphing components, all tuned through a handful of CSS variables.
questions: What JS orchestration applies and removes .flip-freeze around FlipBridge measurements?
How is the transition direction chosen for each pair of screens?
How are shared elements cloned into .flip-layer and reconciled with pointer-events during travel?
Which DOM classes correspond to the 'Vi orb' and 'composer bar' mentioned in the comments?
Does FlipBridge motion still run when prefers-reduced-motion is active, and if so how is it suppressed?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is a directional push transition with shared-element continuity and tunable variables; the file delivers that for three screens, but it also relies on opacity fades for some elements, so the 'transform-only' claim is only mostly true.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make app navigation feel like physical sheets sliding past each other, with persistent controls literally traveling between layouts so the interface reads as one continuous space rather than separate pages fading in place.
novelty: The 'shed' keyframe pattern for sub-parts that lack a counterpart during a morph, and the explicit transparent screen-ghost overlay that lets outgoing and incoming content move simultaneously without crossfading.
contribution: A reusable set of directional slide, fold, pop, and shed keyframes plus screen-specific animation bindings and a CSS-variable tuning surface that a fused system could keep to preserve the app's choreographed transition feel and reduced-motion behavior.
summary_for_embedding: CSS animation choreography file defining directional screen transitions, shared-element travel layers, shed animations, and reduced-motion handling for the Counterpart app's Welcome, Wizard, and Studio screens.
intention_for_embedding: To implement a tunable, transform-biased, directional push transition system with shared-element continuity and graceful degradation under reduced motion or throttled rendering.
